{{Infobox prepared food | name = Yoshida Udon | enname = Yoshida Udon | image = Yoshida udon 001.jpg | caption = Typical "Yoshida Udon" with [[cabbage]] and {{ill|Horse meat|en|Horse meat|WD=Q1124327|lt=Horse meat}}. | alternate_name = | country = {{JPN}} | region = {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|en|Yamanashi Prefecture|WD=Q132720|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}} {{ill|Fujiyoshida|en|Fujiyoshida|WD=Q647508|lt=Fujiyoshida}} and the surrounding {{ill|Gunnai Region|ja|郡内地方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gunnai Region|1=Gunnai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11643105}} | national_cuisine = | creator = | year = | mintime = | maxtime = | type = {{ill|Udon|en|Udon|WD=Q471861|lt=Udon}} | course = | served = | main_ingredient = | minor_ingredient = | variations = | serving_size = | calories = | protein = | fat = | carbohydrate = | glycemic_index = | similar_dish = | other = | complexity = }}
'''Yoshida Udon''' (吉田のうどん) is a type of {{ill|regional cuisine|en|regional cuisine|WD=Q94951|lt=regional cuisine}} from {{ill|Fujiyoshida|en|Fujiyoshida|WD=Q647508|lt=Fujiyoshida}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|en|Yamanashi Prefecture|WD=Q132720|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}}, and its surrounding Gunnai Region. This {{ill|Udon|en|Udon|WD=Q471861|lt=Udon}} dish is characterized by its exceptionally firm, chewy noodles and a distinctive chili-based condiment known as ''suridane''. It was selected as one of the "Top 100 Local Dishes of Rural Japan" by the {{ill|Ministry of Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries|en|Ministry of Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries|WD=Q16877175|lt=Ministry of Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries}} in 2007.
== History ==
Among the traditional wheat-based dishes of Yamanashi Prefecture are {{ill|Hōtō|en|Hōtō|WD=Q2712621|lt=Hōtō}} and Yoshida Udon. These dishes have been staples in the Gunnai Region, including Fujiyoshida. Due to the area's cool climate and volcanic soil, [[rice cultivation]] has been historically difficult. Instead, wheat was grown using a method known as ''mizukake mugi'', which involved protecting the wheat from frost during the cold season by flooding the furrows between rows. This wheat became the foundation of the area's diet, leading to the development of dishes such as {{ill|suiton|en|suiton|WD=Q98320311|lt=suiton}} and later Yoshida Udon.<ref name="yoshidaudon">[https://www.fujiyoshida.net/about/udon.html Yoshida Udon Overview]. Accessed March 22, 2017.</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the region's {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji}} pilgrimage culture flourished, and accommodations such as Yoshida-juku and Kawaguchi-juku developed to cater to pilgrims. Udon was served to these visitors, but it was typically prepared in private homes rather than dedicated shops. To this day, many Yoshida Udon establishments still operate out of residential spaces, with minimal signage.
In the late Edo period through the {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa era}}, Gunnai's main industries included {{ill|Sericulture|en|Sericulture|WD=Q864650|lt=Sericulture}} and [[textile weaving]]. Men, who often worked as traveling merchants, prepared the meals at home, including Yoshida Udon, allowing women to focus on weaving. The firm, chewy texture of Yoshida Udon is attributed to the strength required to knead the dough.<ref name="日経20190314">[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGKKZO42404890T10C19A3KNTP00/ Nikkei Evening Edition, March 14, 2019.] Accessed March 31, 2019.</ref>
== Characteristics ==
=== Noodles === Yoshida Udon noodles are notably firm and chewy, distinguishing them from other udon varieties like {{ill|Hōtō|en|Hōtō|WD=Q2712621|lt=Hōtō}}, which do not rely on gluten for their texture. Common features include: * Thick, square-shaped noodles, often irregularly cut. * Firm texture that requires chewing to break, unlike softer udon. * Hand-rolled noodles often have a twisted appearance after boiling, giving a distinctive volume.
=== Soup === The soup base often uses broth made from [[dried sardines]] or [[:en:shiitake mushrooms|shiitake mushrooms]], with a soy sauce, miso, or blended seasoning depending on the establishment. Toppings like julienned carrots and shiitake slices are common.<ref name="yoshidaudon"/>
=== Toppings === Typical toppings include boiled cabbage, [[:en:Aburaage|Aburaage]] (fried tofu), and sweet-simmered {{ill|Horse meat|en|Horse meat|WD=Q1124327|lt=Horse meat}}, although {{ill|pork|en|pork|WD=Q191768|lt=pork}} is also used. The condiment ''suridane''—a mix of chili, sesame, and oil—is unique to Yoshida Udon and varies by shop.<ref name="日経20190314"/>
== Dining Style ==
In homes, udon is often served for ceremonial occasions. The noodles are presented on a large platter, and diners dip them into individual bowls of warm broth. In shops, popular variations include: * "Meat Udon" with horse or pork meat. * "Cold Noodles" served with chilled broth. * "Tsukemen" (dipping noodles), with an option to keep the noodles warm.
== Shops ==
Fujiyoshida, with a population of about 50,000, has over 60 Yoshida Udon shops, a remarkably high density. Most operate only during lunchtime and close on Sundays. Shops are mapped by local tourism services, and innovations such as fusion dishes and limited-time student-run shops have emerged in recent years.<ref name="日経20190314"/>
== Commercial Products == Yoshida Udon is available as instant noodles, frozen meals, and ready-to-eat dishes in convenience stores.<ref>[https://www.maruchan.co.jp/products/search/2077.html Maruchan Yoshida Udon]</ref>
== Videos == Promotional videos featuring [[:en:Keiji Mutoh|Keiji Mutoh]], a Fujiyoshida native, were produced to highlight Yoshida Udon.<ref>[https://www.fujiyoshida.net/movie/?p=50&pctg=931 Fujiyoshida Udon PR Video]</ref>

{{Infobox prepared food | name = Yoshida Udon | enname = Yoshida Udon | image = Yoshida udon 001.jpg | caption = Typical "Yoshida Udon" with [[cabbage]] and {{ill|Horse meat|en|Horse meat|WD=Q1124327|lt=Horse meat|1=Horse meat}}. | alternate_name = | country = {{JPN}} | region = {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|en|Yamanashi Prefecture|WD=Q132720|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}} {{ill|Fujiyoshida|en|Fujiyoshida|WD=Q647508|lt=Fujiyoshida|1=Fujiyoshida}} and the surrounding {{ill|Gunnai Region|ja|郡内地方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gunnai Region|1=Gunnai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11643105}} | national_cuisine = | creator = | year = | mintime = | maxtime = | type = {{ill|Udon|en|Udon|WD=Q471861|lt=Udon|1=Udon}} | course = | served = | main_ingredient = | minor_ingredient = | variations = | serving_size = | calories = | protein = | fat = | carbohydrate = | glycemic_index = | similar_dish = | other = | complexity = }}
'''Yoshida Udon''' (吉田のうどん) is a type of {{ill|regional cuisine|en|regional cuisine|WD=Q94951|lt=regional cuisine|1=Regional cuisine}} from {{ill|Fujiyoshida|en|Fujiyoshida|WD=Q647508|lt=Fujiyoshida|1=Fujiyoshida}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|en|Yamanashi Prefecture|WD=Q132720|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}}, and its surrounding Gunnai Region. This {{ill|Udon|en|Udon|WD=Q471861|lt=Udon|1=Udon}} dish is characterized by its exceptionally firm, chewy noodles and a distinctive chili-based condiment known as ''suridane''. It was selected as one of the "Top 100 Local Dishes of Rural Japan" by the {{ill|Ministry of Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries|en|Ministry of Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries|WD=Q16877175|lt=Ministry of Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries|1=Ministry of Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries}} in 2007.
== History ==
Among the traditional wheat-based dishes of Yamanashi Prefecture are {{ill|Hōtō|en|Hōtō|WD=Q2712621|lt=Hōtō|1=Hōtō}} and Yoshida Udon. These dishes have been staples in the Gunnai Region, including Fujiyoshida. Due to the area's cool climate and volcanic soil, [[rice cultivation]] has been historically difficult. Instead, wheat was grown using a method known as ''mizukake mugi'', which involved protecting the wheat from frost during the cold season by flooding the furrows between rows. This wheat became the foundation of the area's diet, leading to the development of dishes such as {{ill|suiton|en|suiton|WD=Q98320311|lt=suiton|1=Suiton}} and later Yoshida Udon.<ref name="yoshidaudon">[https://www.fujiyoshida.net/about/udon.html Yoshida Udon Overview]. Accessed March 22, 2017.</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the region's {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji|1=Mount Fuji}} pilgrimage culture flourished, and accommodations such as Yoshida-juku and Kawaguchi-juku developed to cater to pilgrims. Udon was served to these visitors, but it was typically prepared in private homes rather than dedicated shops. To this day, many Yoshida Udon establishments still operate out of residential spaces, with minimal signage.
In the late Edo period through the {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa era|1=Shōwa era}}, Gunnai's main industries included {{ill|Sericulture|en|Sericulture|WD=Q864650|lt=Sericulture|1=Sericulture}} and [[textile weaving]]. Men, who often worked as traveling merchants, prepared the meals at home, including Yoshida Udon, allowing women to focus on weaving. The firm, chewy texture of Yoshida Udon is attributed to the strength required to knead the dough.<ref name="日経20190314">[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGKKZO42404890T10C19A3KNTP00/ Nikkei Evening Edition, March 14, 2019.] Accessed March 31, 2019.</ref>
== Characteristics ==
=== Noodles === Yoshida Udon noodles are notably firm and chewy, distinguishing them from other udon varieties like {{ill|Hōtō|en|Hōtō|WD=Q2712621|lt=Hōtō|1=Hōtō}}, which do not rely on gluten for their texture. Common features include: * Thick, square-shaped noodles, often irregularly cut. * Firm texture that requires chewing to break, unlike softer udon. * Hand-rolled noodles often have a twisted appearance after boiling, giving a distinctive volume.
=== Soup === The soup base often uses broth made from [[dried sardines]] or [[:en:shiitake mushrooms|shiitake mushrooms]], with a soy sauce, miso, or blended seasoning depending on the establishment. Toppings like julienned carrots and shiitake slices are common.<ref name="yoshidaudon"/>
=== Toppings === Typical toppings include boiled cabbage, [[:en:Aburaage|Aburaage]] (fried tofu), and sweet-simmered {{ill|Horse meat|en|Horse meat|WD=Q1124327|lt=Horse meat|1=Horse meat}}, although {{ill|pork|en|pork|WD=Q191768|lt=pork|1=Pork}} is also used. The condiment ''suridane''—a mix of chili, sesame, and oil—is unique to Yoshida Udon and varies by shop.<ref name="日経20190314"/>
== Dining Style ==
In homes, udon is often served for ceremonial occasions. The noodles are presented on a large platter, and diners dip them into individual bowls of warm broth. In shops, popular variations include: * "Meat Udon" with horse or pork meat. * "Cold Noodles" served with chilled broth. * "Tsukemen" (dipping noodles), with an option to keep the noodles warm.
== Shops ==
Fujiyoshida, with a population of about 50,000, has over 60 Yoshida Udon shops, a remarkably high density. Most operate only during lunchtime and close on Sundays. Shops are mapped by local tourism services, and innovations such as fusion dishes and limited-time student-run shops have emerged in recent years.<ref name="日経20190314"/>
== Commercial Products == Yoshida Udon is available as instant noodles, frozen meals, and ready-to-eat dishes in convenience stores.<ref>[https://www.maruchan.co.jp/products/search/2077.html Maruchan Yoshida Udon]</ref>
== Videos == Promotional videos featuring [[:en:Keiji Mutoh|Keiji Mutoh]], a Fujiyoshida native, were produced to highlight Yoshida Udon.<ref>[https://www.fujiyoshida.net/movie/?p=50&pctg=931 Fujiyoshida Udon PR Video]</ref>
== References ==

== External Links == * [https://www.fujiyoshida.net/about/udon.html Yoshida Udon Overview] (Fujiyoshida Tourism Promotion Service)

These dishes have been staples in the Gunnai Region, including Fujiyoshida. Due to the area's cool climate and volcanic soil, [[rice cultivation]] has been historically difficult. Instead, wheat was grown using a method known as ''mizukake mugi'', which involved protecting the wheat from frost during the cold season by flooding the furrows between rows. This wheat became the foundation of the area's diet, leading to the development of dishes such as {{ill|suiton|en|suiton|WD=Q98320311|lt=suiton|1=Suiton}} and later Yoshida Udon.<ref name="yoshidaudon">[https://www.fujiyoshida.net/about/udon.html Yoshida Udon Overview]. Accessed March 22, 2017.</ref>
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the region's {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji|1=Mount Fuji}} pilgrimage culture flourished, and accommodations such as Yoshida-juku and Kawaguchi-juku developed to cater to pilgrims. Udon was served to these visitors, but it was typically prepared in private homes rather than dedicated shops. To this day, many Yoshida Udon establishments still operate out of residential spaces, with minimal signage.
In the late Edo period through the {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa era|1=Shōwa era}}, Gunnai's main industries included {{ill|Sericulture|en|Sericulture|WD=Q864650|lt=Sericulture|1=Sericulture}} and [[textile weaving]]. Men, who often worked as traveling merchants, prepared the meals at home, including Yoshida Udon, allowing women to focus on weaving. The firm, chewy texture of Yoshida Udon is attributed to the strength required to knead the dough.<ref name="日経20190314">[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGKKZO42404890T10C19A3KNTP00/ Nikkei Evening Edition, March 14, 2019.] Accessed March 31, 2019.</ref>
== Characteristics ==
=== Noodles === Yoshida Udon noodles are notably firm and chewy, distinguishing them from other udon varieties like {{ill|Hōtō|en|Hōtō|WD=Q2712621|lt=Hōtō|1=Hōtō}}, which do not rely on gluten for their texture. Common features include: * Thick, square-shaped noodles, often irregularly cut. * Firm texture that requires chewing to break, unlike softer udon. * Hand-rolled noodles often have a twisted appearance after boiling, giving a distinctive volume.
=== Soup === The soup base often uses broth made from [[dried sardines]] or [[:en:shiitake mushrooms|shiitake mushrooms]], with a soy sauce, miso, or blended seasoning depending on the establishment. Toppings like julienned carrots and shiitake slices are common.<ref name="yoshidaudon"/>
=== Toppings === Typical toppings include boiled cabbage, [[:en:Aburaage|Aburaage]] (fried tofu), and sweet-simmered {{ill|Horse meat|en|Horse meat|WD=Q1124327|lt=Horse meat|1=Horse meat}}, although {{ill|pork|en|pork|WD=Q191768|lt=pork|1=Pork}} is also used. The condiment ''suridane''—a mix of chili, sesame, and oil—is unique to Yoshida Udon and varies by shop.<ref name="日経20190314"/>
== Dining Style ==
In homes, udon is often served for ceremonial occasions. The noodles are presented on a large platter, and diners dip them into individual bowls of warm broth. In shops, popular variations include: * "Meat Udon" with horse or pork meat. * "Cold Noodles" served with chilled broth. * "Tsukemen" (dipping noodles), with an option to keep the noodles warm.
== Shops ==
Fujiyoshida, with a population of about 50,000, has over 60 Yoshida Udon shops, a remarkably high density. Most operate only during lunchtime and close on Sundays. Shops are mapped by local tourism services, and innovations such as fusion dishes and limited-time student-run shops have emerged in recent years.<ref name="日経20190314"/>
== Commercial Products == Yoshida Udon is available as instant noodles, frozen meals, and ready-to-eat dishes in convenience stores.<ref>[https://www.maruchan.co.jp/products/search/2077.html Maruchan Yoshida Udon]</ref>
== Videos == Promotional videos featuring [[:en:Keiji Mutoh|Keiji Mutoh]], a Fujiyoshida native, were produced to highlight Yoshida Udon.<ref>[https://www.fujiyoshida.net/movie/?p=50&pctg=931 Fujiyoshida Udon PR Video]</ref>
== References ==

{{Infobox prepared food | name = Yoshida Udon | enname = Yoshida Udon | image = Yoshida udon 001.jpg | caption = Typical "Yoshida Udon" with [[cabbage]] and {{ill|Horse meat|en|Horse meat|WD=Q1124327|lt=Horse meat|1=Horse meat}}. | alternate_name = | country = {{JPN}} | region = {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|en|Yamanashi Prefecture|WD=Q132720|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}} {{ill|Fujiyoshida|en|Fujiyoshida|WD=Q647508|lt=Fujiyoshida|1=Fujiyoshida}} and the surrounding {{ill|Gunnai Region|ja|郡内地方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gunnai Region|1=Gunnai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11643105}} | national_cuisine = | creator = | year = | mintime = | maxtime = | type = {{ill|Udon|en|Udon|WD=Q471861|lt=Udon|1=Udon}} | course = | served = | main_ingredient = | minor_ingredient = | variations = | serving_size = | calories = | protein = | fat = | carbohydrate = | glycemic_index = | similar_dish = | other = | complexity = }}
'''Yoshida Udon''' (吉田のうどん) is a type of {{ill|regional cuisine|en|regional cuisine|WD=Q94951|lt=regional cuisine|1=Regional cuisine}} from {{ill|Fujiyoshida|en|Fujiyoshida|WD=Q647508|lt=Fujiyoshida|1=Fujiyoshida}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|en|Yamanashi Prefecture|WD=Q132720|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}}, and its surrounding Gunnai Region. This {{ill|Udon|en|Udon|WD=Q471861|lt=Udon|1=Udon}} dish is characterized by its exceptionally firm, chewy noodles and a distinctive chili-based condiment known as ''suridane''. It was selected as one of the "Top 100 Local Dishes of Rural Japan" by the {{ill|Ministry of Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries|en|Ministry of Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries|WD=Q16877175|lt=Ministry of Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries|1=Ministry of Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries}} in 2007.
== History ==
Among the traditional wheat-based dishes of Yamanashi Prefecture are {{ill|Hōtō|en|Hōtō|WD=Q2712621|lt=Hōtō|1=Hōtō}} and Yoshida Udon. These dishes have been staples in the Gunnai Region, including Fujiyoshida. Due to the area's cool climate and volcanic soil, [[rice cultivation]] has been historically difficult. Instead, wheat was grown using a method known as ''mizukake mugi'', which involved protecting the wheat from frost during the cold season by flooding the furrows between rows. This wheat became the foundation of the area's diet, leading to the development of dishes such as {{ill|suiton|en|suiton|WD=Q98320311|lt=suiton|1=Suiton}} and later Yoshida Udon.<ref name="yoshidaudon">[https://www.fujiyoshida.net/about/udon.html Yoshida Udon Overview]. Accessed March 22, 2017.</ref>

Udon was served to these visitors, but it was typically prepared in private homes rather than dedicated shops. To this day, many Yoshida Udon establishments still operate out of residential spaces, with minimal signage.
In the late Edo period through the {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa era|1=Shōwa era}}, Gunnai's main industries included {{ill|Sericulture|en|Sericulture|WD=Q864650|lt=Sericulture|1=Sericulture}} and [[textile weaving]]. Men, who often worked as traveling merchants, prepared the meals at home, including Yoshida Udon, allowing women to focus on weaving. The firm, chewy texture of Yoshida Udon is attributed to the strength required to knead the dough.<ref name="日経20190314">[https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGKKZO42404890T10C19A3KNTP00/ Nikkei Evening Edition, March 14, 2019.] Accessed March 31, 2019.</ref>
== Characteristics ==
=== Noodles === Yoshida Udon noodles are notably firm and chewy, distinguishing them from other udon varieties like {{ill|Hōtō|en|Hōtō|WD=Q2712621|lt=Hōtō|1=Hōtō}}, which do not rely on gluten for their texture. Common features include: * Thick, square-shaped noodles, often irregularly cut. * Firm texture that requires chewing to break, unlike softer udon. * Hand-rolled noodles often have a twisted appearance after boiling, giving a distinctive volume.
=== Soup === The soup base often uses broth made from [[dried sardines]] or [[:en:shiitake mushrooms|shiitake mushrooms]], with a soy sauce, miso, or blended seasoning depending on the establishment. Toppings like julienned carrots and shiitake slices are common.<ref name="yoshidaudon"/>
=== Toppings === Typical toppings include boiled cabbage, [[:en:Aburaage|Aburaage]] (fried tofu), and sweet-simmered {{ill|Horse meat|en|Horse meat|WD=Q1124327|lt=Horse meat|1=Horse meat}}, although {{ill|pork|en|pork|WD=Q191768|lt=pork|1=Pork}} is also used. The condiment ''suridane''—a mix of chili, sesame, and oil—is unique to Yoshida Udon and varies by shop.<ref name="日経20190314"/>
== Dining Style ==
In homes, udon is often served for ceremonial occasions. The noodles are presented on a large platter, and diners dip them into individual bowls of warm broth. In shops, popular variations include: * "Meat Udon" with horse or pork meat. * "Cold Noodles" served with chilled broth. * "Tsukemen" (dipping noodles), with an option to keep the noodles warm.
== Shops ==
Fujiyoshida, with a population of about 50,000, has over 60 Yoshida Udon shops, a remarkably high density. Most operate only during lunchtime and close on Sundays. Shops are mapped by local tourism services, and innovations such as fusion dishes and limited-time student-run shops have emerged in recent years.<ref name="日経20190314"/>
